channel.title: Comments for page "The Bard of Ambrose"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Bard of Ambrose"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A work of art. +1
rest in peace best artificial superintelligence

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just discovered this tale, and holy crap it's great. Great characterization, great plot, and all-around well crafted.

+1
Who says nobody reads tales?

item.title: AI done right
item.content:encoded:
I just discovered this tale.
That's an impressive work and, I must say, a rare example of an AGI fiction done right.
In particular, I liked the concept of a "philosophical" AI; I really enjoyed it, and upvoted.

item.title: Happy Birthday Bard
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday marked the 1 year anniversary since posting for this article. Gotta say, it's done much better than I could ever have anticipated.

In the year since this went up, I have written a sort-of-sequel, in the form of scp-2897. If you ever thought that sad computer couldn't get any sadder, think again. And then cry.

item.title: Note to self; read more of the Third Law Hub
item.content:encoded:
Oh, why the hell has it taken me this long to read this story? A story I have been yearning for; one where an A.I becomes sapient and doesn't go murder-happy over some philosophical quandry it can't figure out, a story about an A.I that actually feels (to me, anyways) like an intelligent A.I. That makes me feel all kinds of giddy!

And, well, depressed, of course, given the ending, which I can only give praise even if I can't really articulate it well beyond what others have stated or at least implied: you were able to make us feel bad for something that doesn't even care if it is something special or not. That's—well, when I describe it like that, not the most Olympian of challenges, but an accomplishment nonetheless, and one that I adore.

So, yeah, if it's not obvious at this point, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Fanart (again)
- THIS MACHINE KILLS FEELS

Note to self: Sad computers = more upvotes.

In all seriousness, thanks to everyone who voted on this tale, even the downvoters. It's a great feeling, knowing that other people are reading and enjoying something you spent so much time on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was funny, the first time I read the Chinese Room argument, I thought, silly, that's what we're doing right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am glad I am not the only person who read the Chinese Room and reached the conclusion that it merely proves that "consciousness" as Searle defines it can't exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An AI claiming to be nonsapient…that was new. And really, really good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must have thought I did lol. I did now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But you didn't upvote…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ

That was… Powerful. Great work.

+1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Minor update: I removed the collapsible/stinger at the very end, as it's not really needed. It was originally written to fill some plot holes that were later filled by the relevant sections being rewritten, and without it I think the tale works better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A company specializing in combat robots you say…

In all seriousness, this was a thoroughly enjoyable tale. The characters were interesting, and Bard was particularly fun to read.

+1 GW. This is certainly a great launch pad for so many things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic view on what a "sapient" A.I. is like in my opinion. It's self-thinking and self-aware, but it doesn't let that conflict with the fact that it's, well, a computer. It believes it's just following it's protocols and set directions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already like where this is going. If this continues as great as it started it could become one of my favourite canons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow this was sad as all hell.
I really liked this tale.
It's a great concept and a great debate on philosophy and AIs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Computers dying is always sad, kind of like pets. ;_;

And then you stick new circuit boards in them and bring them back to life!
(Works better with computers, though.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job! I really loved this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Computers dying is always sad, kind of like pets. ;_; This was really good, and I can't wait to see what comes next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. And it works both ways: there's no reason a sufficiently complicated set of pullies and gears running a set of Chinese Rooms couldn't be sapient if it is sufficiently far beyond the Edge of Chaos. To paraphrase B.F. Skinner in a way he would probably have hated: The real question is not whether men think, but at what point an algorithm begins to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Searle actually doesn't go quite that far, because he tries to argue that only digital brains (i.e. computers) are incapable of consciousness. Although even a cursory examination of his logic shows that he has no real reason or basis for not extending that argument to all thinking creatures. Which is what Bard does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, Searle… "this novel thought I am having is neither novel nor a thought"
Should have let it read Kant and Lorenz too. X3

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
You are all zombies.

This tale is the first tale written for what will hopefully eventually be the "Third Law" canon.

Critique credits and thanks (in no particular order): Conwell, Cimmerian, TwistedGears, TyGently, minmin, kola, ParaDLell, EmberTech, thomas, Decibelle, A_Random_Day, and ChaoSera.
